questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper explicitly mentions pre-training on ImageNet-21k and JFT-300M. It also reports evaluation on ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, which has 87M parameters. The paper’s reference ViT/DeiT-B variant is 86M, but the biggest reported variant in the evidence is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, which gives linear computational complexity with respect to image size and makes it suitable as a general-purpose vision backbone. Relative position bias is another architectural choice noted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network learns to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus reports a very wide range of ImageNet top-1 accuracies, from 1.3 to 91.0 (spread 89.7), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower top claims such as PaLI-17B at 90.9 and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest value in the evidence is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), and no explicit SOTA claims are listed in the provided evidence.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows conflicting SOTA claims on ADE20K mIoU: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is also repeatedly claimed as SOTA at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is reported at 59.9. So the papers do not agree on a single state-of-the-art model, and the reported best value in the corpus is 62.9.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus reports a wide range of ADE20K mIoU values, from 26.6 to 62.9 (spread 36.3, stddev 7.29), so the papers do not agree on a single reported value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is listed at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, indicating different papers claim top performance with different numbers.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design, e.g. ViT says it "adds position embeddings" and CPVT says vision transformers commonly use positional encodings, while others argue they are not necessary or can be removed with little harm: CvT says it can "safely remove positional encoding without degradation," and a self-supervised ViT study says removing position embedding "only slightly hurts performance." There is also a middle ground: Swin Transformer reports that absolute position embedding can "slightly hurt dense prediction tasks," so relative position bias is preferred there.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and peaked at 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to 2022, then down through 2024.
